mappings.rationaleAI: Directly addresses bioelectricity (B) through neurons transmitting electrical and chemical signals, neuron structure and sensory/motor/interneuron subdivisions (C.1), the nature of the nerve impulse (C.3), and synaptic transmission via neurotransmitters including acetylcholine and dopamine (C.4).

The cell as the fundamental structural unit of life involves cell membrane structure and function (A.1) and organelle compartmentalization (A.2), with cellular processes such as metabolism and signaling dependent on membrane-mediated selective transport.

Mitochondria are membrane-bound organelles exemplifying cellular compartmentalization (A.2), with their double-membrane structure (A.1) essential to ATP production through chemiosmotic gradients across the inner membrane.

Explicitly quantifies neuron structure with 100,000 neurons, 2 million axons, and 1 billion synapses per grain-of-sand-sized brain tissue (C.1), directly illustrating the molecular complexity of nerve impulse transmission (C.3) and synaptic connectivity (C.4).

Galvani's experiments making dead frogs' legs twitch with electricity directly demonstrate bioelectricity (B) and muscle contraction (D), while the broader inquiry into what animates living matter connects to contractile activity as a characteristic of all living things (D.1).

Slime moulds exhibit collective motile and contractile activity without nervous systems, directly illustrating that contractile or motile activity is a fundamental characteristic of all living things (D.1), not restricted to organisms with specialized muscle tissue.
